question_text: Consider the so-called 'D-T reaction' (Deuterium-Tritium reaction). solution: {tex}\Delta m =\left[m\left({ }_{1}^{2} H\right)+m\left({ }_{1}^{3} H\right)\right]-{/tex} {tex}\left[m\left({ }_{2}^{4} H e\right)+m\left({ }_{0}^{1} {n}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=(2.014102+3.016049)-{/tex} {tex}(4.002603+1.008665){/tex} {tex}=0.018883 {u}{/tex} {tex}{Q} =\Delta m \times 931{/tex} {tex}=0.018883 \times 931 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}{Q}=17.58 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}\text { Nuclear density }=\frac{\text { Mass of nucleus }}{\text { Volume of nucleus }}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}{/tex} {tex}R=R_{0} A^{1 / 3}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_{0}^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Independent of mass number (A) }{/tex}

{tex}=(2.014102+3.016049)-{/tex}{tex}(4.002603+1.008665){/tex}
{tex}=0.018883 {u}{/tex}
{tex}{Q} =\Delta m \times 931{/tex}
{tex}=0.018883 \times 931 {MeV}{/tex}

{tex}\text { Nuclear density }=\frac{\text { Mass of nucleus }}{\text { Volume of nucleus }}{/tex}

question_text: Define 'Mass defect' and 'Binding energy' of a nucleus. Describe 'Fission process' on the basis of binding energy per nucleon. A deuteron contains a proton and a neutron and has a mass of 2.013553 u. Calculate the mass defect for it in u and its energy equivalence in MeV. ( {tex}{m}_{{p}}=1.007277 {u}, {/tex} {tex}{m}_{{n}}=1.008665 {u}, 1 {u}=931.5 {MeV} / {c}^{2}{/tex} )
explanation: Difference in the mass of the nucleus and its constituents is defined as mass defect. Binding Energy is the energy required to separate the nucleons from the nucleus. In Fission process a heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei and energy is released. As a result the Binding Energy per nucleon increases. {tex}\Delta {m}=\left({m}_{{p}}+{m}_{{n}}\right)-{m}_{{d}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m}=(1.007277+1.008665){/tex} {tex}-2.013553{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m}=0.002389 {u}{/tex} Energy released {tex}=\Delta {m} \times {c}^{2}{/tex} Energy released {tex}=0.002389 \times 931.5{/tex} {tex}=2.2253 {MeV} \approx 2.22 {MeV}{/tex}
solution: Difference in the mass of the nucleus and its constituents is defined as mass defect. Binding Energy is the energy required to separate the nucleons from the nucleus. In Fission process a heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei and energy is released. As a result the Binding Energy per nucleon increases. {tex}\Delta {m}=\left({m}_{{p}}+{m}_{{n}}\right)-{m}_{{d}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m}=(1.007277+1.008665){/tex} {tex}-2.013553{/tex} {tex}\Delta {m}=0.002389 {u}{/tex} Energy released {tex}=\Delta {m} \times {c}^{2}{/tex} Energy released {tex}=0.002389 \times 931.5{/tex} {tex}=2.2253 {MeV} \approx 2.22 {MeV}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Difference in the mass of the nucleus and its constituents is defined as mass defect.
    Binding Energy is the energy required to separate the nucleons from the nucleus.
    In Fission process a heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei and energy is released. As a result the Binding Energy per nucleon increases.
  2. {tex}\Delta {m}=\left({m}_{{p}}+{m}_{{n}}\right)-{m}_{{d}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {m}=(1.007277+1.008665){/tex}{tex}-2.013553{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {m}=0.002389 {u}{/tex}
    Energy released {tex}=\Delta {m} \times {c}^{2}{/tex}
    Energy released {tex}=0.002389 \times 931.5{/tex}
    {tex}=2.2253 {MeV} \approx 2.22 {MeV}{/tex}

1. State any two properties of a nucleus.
  2. Why is the density of a nucleus much more than that of an atom?
  3. Show that the density of the nuclear matter is the same for all nuclei.
question_text: State any two properties of a nucleus. Why is the density of a nucleus much more than that of an atom? Show that the density of the nuclear matter is the same for all nuclei.
explanation: Properties of a Nucleus Small Size: The nucleus is extremely small compared to the atom, with a radius on the order of 10 -15 meters (femtometers), while the atom is around 10 -10 meters in size. Positive Charge: The nucleus carries a positive charge due to protons, which attracts the negatively charged electrons, thus holding the atom together. Density Comparison: The density of a nucleus is much higher than that of an atom because the nucleus is concentrated in a much smaller volume. The entire mass of the atom, primarily concentrated in the nucleus, is packed into this tiny volume, resulting in much higher density. The electron cloud surrounding the nucleus occupies a much larger volume, contributing less to the overall mass density of the atom. Nuclear Matter Density The volume V of a nucleus is proportional to A, where A is the mass number: {tex}V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A{/tex} The density {tex}\rho{/tex} of the nucleus is: {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{A \times m_{\text {nucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A}=\frac{m_{\text {mucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3}{/tex} Since m nucleon and R 0 are constants, {tex}\rho{/tex} is constant for all nuclei, showing that nuclear matter density is roughly the same across different nuclei.
solution: Properties of a Nucleus Small Size: The nucleus is extremely small compared to the atom, with a radius on the order of 10 -15 meters (femtometers), while the atom is around 10 -10 meters in size. Positive Charge: The nucleus carries a positive charge due to protons, which attracts the negatively charged electrons, thus holding the atom together. Density Comparison: The density of a nucleus is much higher than that of an atom because the nucleus is concentrated in a much smaller volume. The entire mass of the atom, primarily concentrated in the nucleus, is packed into this tiny volume, resulting in much higher density. The electron cloud surrounding the nucleus occupies a much larger volume, contributing less to the overall mass density of the atom. Nuclear Matter Density The volume V of a nucleus is proportional to A, where A is the mass number: {tex}V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A{/tex} The density {tex}\rho{/tex} of the nucleus is: {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{A \times m_{\text {nucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A}=\frac{m_{\text {mucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3}{/tex} Since m nucleon and R 0 are constants, {tex}\rho{/tex} is constant for all nuclei, showing that nuclear matter density is roughly the same across different nuclei.
solution_html:
1. Properties of a Nucleus
    1. Small Size: The nucleus is extremely small compared to the atom, with a radius on the order of 10-15 meters (femtometers), while the atom is around 10-10 meters in size.
    2. Positive Charge: The nucleus carries a positive charge due to protons, which attracts the negatively charged electrons, thus holding the atom together.
  2. Density Comparison: The density of a nucleus is much higher than that of an atom because the nucleus is concentrated in a much smaller volume. The entire mass of the atom, primarily concentrated in the nucleus, is packed into this tiny volume, resulting in much higher density. The electron cloud surrounding the nucleus occupies a much larger volume, contributing less to the overall mass density of the atom.
  3. Nuclear Matter Density
    The volume V of a nucleus is proportional to A, where A is the mass number:
    {tex}V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A{/tex}
    The density {tex}\rho{/tex} of the nucleus is:
    {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{A \times m_{\text {nucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3 A}=\frac{m_{\text {mucleon }}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_0^3}{/tex}
    Since mnucleon and R0 are constants, {tex}\rho{/tex} is constant for all nuclei, showing that nuclear matter density is roughly the same across different nuclei.

question_text: Briefly discuss three characteristics of the forces between nucleons. Which out of {tex}{ }_4^8 {X}{/tex} and {tex}{ }_3^5 {Y}{/tex} nuclei is more stable and why?
explanation: Characteristics of Forces Between Nucleons Short-Range Nature : The nucleon-nucleon force is a strong interaction that acts over a very short range, typically about 1 femtometer (10 -15 meters). Beyond this range, the force rapidly decreases, becoming negligible. Strong and Attractive : The force between nucleons is extremely strong, significantly stronger than electromagnetic forces. It is predominantly attractive, which helps bind protons and neutrons in the nucleus against the repulsive electromagnetic force between protons. Charge Independence : The nucleon-nucleon force is approximately independent of the charge of the nucleons. This means that the force between a proton and a neutron is similar to that between two neutrons or two protons, allowing for various combinations of nucleons in a stable nucleus. {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} is more stable The ratio of number of neutrons to the number of protons is more in {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} than {tex}{ }_3^5 Y{/tex}

Beyond this range, the force rapidly decreases, becoming negligible. Strong and Attractive : The force between nucleons is extremely strong, significantly stronger than electromagnetic forces. It is predominantly attractive, which helps bind protons and neutrons in the nucleus against the repulsive electromagnetic force between protons. Charge Independence : The nucleon-nucleon force is approximately independent of the charge of the nucleons. This means that the force between a proton and a neutron is similar to that between two neutrons or two protons, allowing for various combinations of nucleons in a stable nucleus. {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} is more stable The ratio of number of neutrons to the number of protons is more in {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} than {tex}{ }_3^5 Y{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Characteristics of Forces Between Nucleons
    1. Short-Range Nature: The nucleon-nucleon force is a strong interaction that acts over a very short range, typically about 1 femtometer (10-15 meters). Beyond this range, the force rapidly decreases, becoming negligible.
    2. Strong and Attractive: The force between nucleons is extremely strong, significantly stronger than electromagnetic forces. It is predominantly attractive, which helps bind protons and neutrons in the nucleus against the repulsive electromagnetic force between protons.
    3. Charge Independence: The nucleon-nucleon force is approximately independent of the charge of the nucleons. This means that the force between a proton and a neutron is similar to that between two neutrons or two protons, allowing for various combinations of nucleons in a stable nucleus.
  2. {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} is more stable
    The ratio of number of neutrons to the number of protons is more in {tex}{ }_4^8 X{/tex} than {tex}{ }_3^5 Y{/tex}

question_text: Write two characteristic properties of nuclear force. Draw a plot of potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation. Write two important conclusions that can be drawn from the plot.
explanation: Properties of nuclear forces: The nuclear force is much stronger than the Coulomb force acting between charges or the gravitational forces between their masses. The nuclear force between two nucleons falls rapidly to zero as their distance becomes more than a few femtometres. Conclusions:- The potential energy is minimum at a distance r. The force between the nucleons is attractive for distances larger than r and repulsive if they are separated by distance less than r.

question_text: Calculate the energy released/absorbed (in MeV) in the nuclear reaction: {tex}{ }_{1}^{1} {H}+{ }_{1}^{3} {H} \longrightarrow{ }_{1}^{2} {H}+{ }_{1}^{2} {H}{/tex} Given : {tex}{m}\left({ }_{1}^{1} {H}\right)=1.007825 \mu{/tex} {tex}{m}\left({ }_{1}^{2} {H}\right)=2.014102 \mu{/tex} {tex}{~m}\left({ }_{1}^{3} {H}\right)=3.016049 \mu{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\text { Mass of reactants }= (1.007825+3.016049) {u}{/tex} {tex}= 4.023874 {u}{/tex} {tex}\text { Mass of product }= 2 \times 2.014102 {u}{/tex} {tex}=4.028204 {u}{/tex} {tex}\text { Mass defect, } \Delta {m}= 4.023874 {u}-4.028204 {u}{/tex} {tex}=-0.00433 {u}{/tex} As the mass defect is negative, energy is absorbed. Energy absorbed, {tex}{E}=0.00433 \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}=4.03 {MeV}{/tex}

Energy absorbed, {tex}{E}=0.00433 \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex} {tex}=4.03 {MeV}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Mass of reactants }= (1.007825+3.016049) {u}{/tex}
{tex}= 4.023874 {u}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Mass of product }= 2 \times 2.014102 {u}{/tex}
{tex}=4.028204 {u}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Mass defect, } \Delta {m}= 4.023874 {u}-4.028204 {u}{/tex}
{tex}=-0.00433 {u}{/tex}
As the mass defect is negative, energy is absorbed.
Energy absorbed, {tex}{E}=0.00433 \times 931.5 {MeV}{/tex}

question_text: How is the size of a nucleus found experimentally? Write the relation between the radius and mass number of a nucleus. Prove that the density of a nucleus is independent of its mass number.
explanation: Size of nucleus of an atom is determined by scattering experiments in which fast electrons are used to bombard targets. Relation between radius and mass number of nucleus. R = R 0 A 1/3 Density of nucleus {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m \times A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{1 / 3}\right)^3}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0^3}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that density of nucleus is independent of mass number (A).
solution: Size of nucleus of an atom is determined by scattering experiments in which fast electrons are used to bombard targets. Relation between radius and mass number of nucleus. R = R 0 A 1/3 Density of nucleus {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m \times A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{1 / 3}\right)^3}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0^3}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that density of nucleus is independent of mass number (A).

1. Size of nucleus of an atom is determined by scattering experiments in which fast electrons are used to bombard targets.
    Relation between radius and mass number of nucleus.
    R = R0A1/3
  2. Density of nucleus
    {tex}\rho=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}{/tex}
    {tex}\rho=\frac{m \times A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}{/tex}
    {tex}\rho=\frac{m A}{\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(R_0 A^{1 / 3}\right)^3}{/tex}
    {tex}\rho=\frac{3 m}{4 \pi R_0^3}{/tex}
    Hence, it is proved that density of nucleus is independent of mass number (A).

question_html: Draw a diagram to show the variation of binding energy per nucleon with mass number for different nuclei and mention its two features. Why do lighter nuclei usually undergo nuclear fusion?
question_text: Draw a diagram to show the variation of binding energy per nucleon with mass number for different nuclei and mention its two features. Why do lighter nuclei usually undergo nuclear fusion?
explanation: Features of diagram Binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number (30 < A < 170) The curve has maximum of about 8.75 MeV for A= 56 and has a value of 7.6 MeV for A = 238 Binding energy per nucleon is lower for both light nuclei (A > 170) Two lighter nuclei fuse together to form heavier nuclei as the binding energy per nucleon of fused heavier nuclei is more than the binding energy per nucleon of the lighter nuclei. Thus the final system is more tightly bound than initial system in order to acheive more stability.
solution: Features of diagram Binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number (30 < A < 170) The curve has maximum of about 8.75 MeV for A= 56 and has a value of 7.6 MeV for A = 238 Binding energy per nucleon is lower for both light nuclei (A > 170) Two lighter nuclei fuse together to form heavier nuclei as the binding energy per nucleon of fused heavier nuclei is more than the binding energy per nucleon of the lighter nuclei. Thus the final system is more tightly bound than initial system in order to acheive more stability.
solution_html:
Features of diagram

  1. Binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number (30 < A < 170)
  2. The curve has maximum of about 8.75 MeV for A= 56 and has a value of 7.6 MeV for A = 238
  3. Binding energy per nucleon is lower for both light nuclei (A > 170)

Two lighter nuclei fuse together to form heavier nuclei as the binding energy per nucleon of fused heavier nuclei is more than the binding energy per nucleon of the lighter nuclei. Thus the final system is more tightly bound than initial system in order to acheive more stability.

question_text: Distinguish between nuclear fission and fusion giving an example of each. Explain the release of energy in nuclear fission and fusion on the basis of binding energy per nucleon curve.
explanation: Nuclear fission - It is a process in which a heavy nucleus when excited (say on bombarding by a slow moving neutron) splits into two lighter nuclei of nearly comparable masses with a release of large amount of energy. Example of nuclear fission {tex}{ }_0^1 n+{ }_{92}^{235} U \rightarrow{ }_{92}^{236} U \rightarrow{ }_{56}^{144} B a+{ }_{36}^{89} K r+3{ }_0^1 n+Q{/tex} Nuclear Fusion - It is a process in which two lighter nuclei fuse (at extremely high temperature) to form a heavy nucleus and large amount of energy is released. Examples of nuclear fusion {tex}{ }_1^1 H+{ }_1^1 H \rightarrow{ }_1^2 H+e^{+}+v+Q_1{/tex} {tex}{ }_1^2 H+{ }_1^2 H \rightarrow{ }_2^3 H+n+Q_2{/tex} {tex}{ }_1^2 H+{ }_1^2 H \rightarrow{ }_1^3 H+{ }_1^1 H+Q_3{/tex} The binding energy per nucleon of the products in the nuclear reactions (nuclear fission and nuclear fusion) is greater than that of the reactants.

question_text: James Chadwick, in 1932, studied the emission of neutral radiations when Beryllium nuclei were bombarded with alpha particles. He concluded that emitted radiations were neutrons and not photons. Explain. Two nuclei may have the same radius, even though they contain different number of protons and neutrons. Explain.
explanation: In 1932 James Chadwick studied the emission of neutral radiations when beryllium nuclei were bombarded with alpha particles. He studied the bombardment of alpha particles and a penetrating radiation out of the beryllium and when this radiation was incident on wax it was emitting out protons by detector on the other side. If the radiations were gamma rays or photons then to make protons emit from wax required huge energy which is not possible at the time with the photons. Also according to conservation of energy and momentum he concluded the radiations to be of neutral charge but mass as very much similar as proton and named as neutron. Neutons and protons together are called as nucleons as they reside inside the nucleus. They have approximately same mass. If two nuclei have different number of protons and neutrons, but total number of nucleons are same i.e. mass number is same they can have same radius. As radius of the nucleus r {tex}r \propto A^{y_3}{/tex} Where A is the mass number.

1. In 1932 James Chadwick studied the emission of neutral radiations when beryllium nuclei were bombarded with alpha particles. He studied the bombardment of alpha particles and a penetrating radiation out of the beryllium and when this radiation was incident on wax it was emitting out protons by detector on the other side. If the radiations were gamma rays or photons then to make protons emit from wax required huge energy which is not possible at the time with the photons. Also according to conservation of energy and momentum he concluded the radiations to be of neutral charge but mass as very much similar as proton and named as neutron.

question_html:
The nucleus {tex}{ }^{235}_{92} \mathrm{Y}{/tex}, initially at rest, decays into {tex}{ }^{231}_{90} \mathrm{X}{/tex} by emitting an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle
{tex}{}_{92}^{235}Y \to {}_{90}^{231}X + {}_2^4He + energy{/tex}
The binding energies per nucleon of the parent nucleus, the daughter nucleus and {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are 7.8 MeV, 7.835 MeV and 7.07 MeV, respectively. Assuming the daughter nucleus to be formed in the unexcited state and neglecting its share in the energy of the reaction, find the speed of the emitted {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. (Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle = 6.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg).
question_text: The nucleus {tex}{ }^{235}_{92} \mathrm{Y}{/tex} , initially at rest, decays into {tex}{ }^{231}_{90} \mathrm{X}{/tex} by emitting an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle {tex}{}_{92}^{235}Y \to {}_{90}^{231}X + {}_2^4He + energy{/tex} The binding energies per nucleon of the parent nucleus, the daughter nucleus and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle are 7.8 MeV, 7.835 MeV and 7.07 MeV, respectively. Assuming the daughter nucleus to be formed in the unexcited state and neglecting its share in the energy of the reaction, find the speed of the emitted {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. (Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle = 6.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg).
explanation: Total B.E of {tex}{ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{Y}{/tex} = 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 235 = 1833 MeV Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{90}^{231} \mathrm{X}{/tex} = 7.835 {tex}\times{/tex} 231 = 1809.9 MeV Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex} = 7.076 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 28.28 MeV Energy released in the decay, Q Q = B.E of X + B.E of He - B.E of Y = 1809.9 + 28.28 - 1833 = 5.18 MeV K.E of a particle, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = Q {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of the emitted {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle is given by, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 Q}{m}}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5.18 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{6.68 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} v = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1
solution: Total B.E of {tex}{ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{Y}{/tex} = 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 235 = 1833 MeV Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{90}^{231} \mathrm{X}{/tex} = 7.835 {tex}\times{/tex} 231 = 1809.9 MeV Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex} = 7.076 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 28.28 MeV Energy released in the decay, Q Q = B.E of X + B.E of He - B.E of Y = 1809.9 + 28.28 - 1833 = 5.18 MeV K.E of a particle, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = Q {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of the emitted {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle is given by, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 Q}{m}}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5.18 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{6.68 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} v = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1
solution_html:
Total B.E of {tex}{ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{Y}{/tex} = 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 235 = 1833 MeV
Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{90}^{231} \mathrm{X}{/tex} = 7.835 {tex}\times{/tex} 231 = 1809.9 MeV
Total B.E. of {tex}{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}{/tex} = 7.076 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 28.28 MeV
Energy released in the decay, Q
Q = B.E of X + B.E of He - B.E of Y
= 1809.9 + 28.28 - 1833
= 5.18 MeV
K.E of a particle, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = Q
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of the emitted {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle is given by, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 Q}{m}}{/tex} 
v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5.18 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{6.68 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} 
v = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 ms-1

question_html:
1. The density of the nuclear matter is tremendously larger than the physical density of the material. Explain.
  2. The nuclear forces are not coulomb forces between nucleons. Explain.
  3. Draw a plot of the potential energy between a pair of nucleons as a function of distance between them inside a nucleus.
question_text: The density of the nuclear matter is tremendously larger than the physical density of the material. Explain. The nuclear forces are not coulomb forces between nucleons. Explain. Draw a plot of the potential energy between a pair of nucleons as a function of distance between them inside a nucleus.
explanation: The density of nuclear matter is approximately 2.3×10 17 kgm -3 which is tremendously larger than the density of ordinary material, say water, which is 10 3 kgm -3 . This is due to the fact that most of an atom is empty and its whole mass is concentrated at its nucleus. Ordinary material, consisting of atoms, has a large amount of empty space and consequently its density is small. Coulombian force between two protons is repulsive. However, within a nucleus a number of protons and neutrons exist together within a very small space. So it is clear that nuclear force is not coulomb force but is an extremely short range force which is attractive in nature and is responsible for maintaining all the nucleons together.

question_html: Draw the curve showing the variation of binding energy per nucleon with the mass number of nuclei. Using it explain the fusion of nuclei lying on ascending part and fission of nuclei lying on descending part of this curve.
question_text: Draw the curve showing the variation of binding energy per nucleon with the mass number of nuclei. Using it explain the fusion of nuclei lying on ascending part and fission of nuclei lying on descending part of this curve.
explanation: Explanation for fusion On the ascending part of the curve B.E./A increases with mass number (A). So, B.E. of the resultant will be greater than that of the nuclei which are fused together. Hence the fusion of the nuclei on the ascending part, results in an increase in binding energy i.e. a more stable nucleus is obtained on fusing two nuclei. Explanation for fission On the descending part of the curve, the B.E. of the heavier nuclei is lower . Hence the fission of the heavier nuclei, on the descending part, will again cause an increase in B.E. and, therefore, more stable nucleus.
solution: Explanation for fusion On the ascending part of the curve B.E./A increases with mass number (A). So, B.E. of the resultant will be greater than that of the nuclei which are fused together. question_html: How are protons, which are positively charged, held together inside a nucleus? Explain the variation of the potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation. State the significance of negative potential energy in this region?
question_text: How are protons, which are positively charged, held together inside a nucleus? Explain the variation of the potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation. State the significance of negative potential energy in this region?
explanation: There are two types of forces: Coulomb force and nuclear force which are acting between protons in the nucleus. The gravitational force between two protons is very weak. Between these two forces, nuclear force is stronger as compared to Coulomb force but it is a very short-range force. Coulomb force is repulsive in nature for two protons dominant over nuclear force when the distance between two protons increases more than a few femtometer. As the nuclear size is in the order of the femtometer, hence in the nucleus, nuclear force between protons dominates over the Columbian force. It is attractive in nature and binds together. Moreover, neutrons in nucleus also act as glue to held protons together as there is no Coulomb force between protons and neutrons but there is an attractive nuclear force between them. It is clear from the graph, as the nucleons come closer under attractive nuclear force, the potential energy decreases and PE becomes more and more negative. When the distance between two nucleons falls below 10 -15 m, the nuclear force becomes repulsive and potential energy increases rapidly. Hence negative PE shows that force between nucleons is attractive. The characteristic properties of nuclear force: The nuclear force is short-range force. The nuclear force is independent of electric charge.

question_text: Explain the processes of nuclear fission and nuclear fusion by using the plot of binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) versus the mass number A. A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 10 years. How long will it take for the activity to reduce to 3.125%?
explanation: The plot of binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) versus the mass number A is given below: During nuclear fission: A heavy nucleus in the larger mass region (A > 200) breaks into two middle level nuclei, resulting in an increase in B.E/ nucleon. This results in a release of energy. Example: {tex}92{{\text{U}}^{235}} + {{\text{n}}^1} \to {}_{56}{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{144}} + {}_{36}{\text{K}}{{\text{r}}^{89}} + {3_0}{{\text{n}}^1} + {\text{ energy }}{/tex} During nuclear fusion: Light nuclei in the lower mass region (A < 20) fuse to form a nucleus having higher B.E/nucleon. Hence Energy gets released. {tex}{}_{\text{1}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{}_{\text{1}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{2}}} \to {}_{\text{2}}{\text{H}}{{\text{e}}^{\text{4}}}{\text{ + energy}}{/tex} Let the initial activity be A o . Let the time taken for the activity to reduce = t. Half live is given T 1/2 = 10 years If we take A o = 100, then activity after time t will be A t = 3.125 (given) We know the law of radioactivity decay is given as A t = A o e - {tex}\lambda{/tex} t ….(i) Where {tex}\lambda{/tex} = decay constant. {tex}\lambda=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{T}_{\frac{1}{2}}}=\frac{0.693}{10}=0.0693{/tex} Therefore, substituting the values of A t , A o and {tex}\lambda{/tex} in equation (i), {tex}3.125=100 e^{-0.0693 t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3.125}{100}=e^{-0.0693 t}{/tex} Taking natural logarithm on both sides of above equation {tex}\ln \left(\frac{3.125}{100}\right)=-0.0693 t{/tex} {tex}\ln \left(\frac{100}{3.125}\right)=0.0693 \mathrm{t}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{\ln 32}{0.0693}=50 \mathrm{years}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The time taken for the activity to reduce to 3.125 % is 50 years.

solution_html:
1.  The plot of binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) versus the mass number A is given below:
    1. During nuclear fission:
      A heavy nucleus in the larger mass region (A > 200) breaks into two middle level nuclei, resulting in an increase in B.E/ nucleon. This results in a release of energy.
      Example:
      {tex}92{{\text{U}}^{235}} + {{\text{n}}^1} \to {}_{56}{\text{B}}{{\text{a}}^{144}} + {}_{36}{\text{K}}{{\text{r}}^{89}} + {3_0}{{\text{n}}^1} + {\text{ energy }}{/tex}
    2. During nuclear fusion:
      Light nuclei in the lower mass region (A < 20) fuse to form a nucleus having higher B.E/nucleon. Hence Energy gets released.
      {tex}{}_{\text{1}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{}_{\text{1}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{2}}} \to {}_{\text{2}}{\text{H}}{{\text{e}}^{\text{4}}}{\text{ + energy}}{/tex}
  2. Let the initial activity be Ao.
    Let the time taken for the activity to reduce = t.
    Half live is given T1/2 = 10 years
    If we take Ao = 100, then activity after time t will be
    At = 3.125 (given)
    We know the law of radioactivity decay is given as
    At = Ao e-{tex}\lambda{/tex}t ….(i)
    Where {tex}\lambda{/tex} = decay constant.
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{T}_{\frac{1}{2}}}=\frac{0.693}{10}=0.0693{/tex}
    Therefore, substituting the values of At, Ao and {tex}\lambda{/tex} in equation (i),
    {tex}3.125=100 e^{-0.0693 t}{/tex}  
    {tex}\frac{3.125}{100}=e^{-0.0693 t}{/tex}
    Taking natural logarithm on both sides of above equation
    {tex}\ln \left(\frac{3.125}{100}\right)=-0.0693 t{/tex} 
    {tex}\ln \left(\frac{100}{3.125}\right)=0.0693 \mathrm{t}{/tex}

question_text: A nucleus with mass number A = 240 and BE/A = 7.6 MeV breaks into two fragments each of A = 120 with BE/ A = 8.5 MeV. Calculate the released energy.
explanation: Let the parent nucleus was P whose binding energy per nucleon was 7.6 MeV which decays into daughter nucleus Q having binding energy per nucleon is 8.5 MeV. According to question, {tex}P \to Q + Q{/tex} BE/A of element P = 7.6 MeV (given) So, {tex}\mathrm { BE } \text { of } P = 7.6 \times 240 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} BE/A of element Q = 8.5 MeV So, BE of {tex}Q = 8.5 \times 120 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} Now, energy released = 2(BE of Q) - BE of P {tex}= 8.5 \times 120 \times 2 - 7.6 \times 240{/tex} {tex}= 2040 - 1824{/tex} = 216 MeV
solution: Let the parent nucleus was P whose binding energy per nucleon was 7.6 MeV which decays into daughter nucleus Q having binding energy per nucleon is 8.5 MeV. According to question, {tex}P \to Q + Q{/tex} BE/A of element P = 7.6 MeV (given) So, {tex}\mathrm { BE } \text { of } P = 7.6 \times 240 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} BE/A of element Q = 8.5 MeV So, BE of {tex}Q = 8.5 \times 120 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} Now, energy released = 2(BE of Q) - BE of P {tex}= 8.5 \times 120 \times 2 - 7.6 \times 240{/tex} {tex}= 2040 - 1824{/tex} = 216 MeV
solution_html:
Let the parent nucleus was P whose binding energy per nucleon was 7.6 MeV which decays into daughter nucleus Q having binding energy per nucleon is 8.5 MeV. According to question,
{tex}P \to Q + Q{/tex}
BE/A of element P = 7.6 MeV (given)
So, {tex}\mathrm { BE } \text { of } P = 7.6 \times 240 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} 
BE/A of element Q = 8.5 MeV 
So, BE of {tex}Q = 8.5 \times 120 \mathrm { MeV }{/tex}
Now, energy released = 2(BE of Q) - BE of P
{tex}= 8.5 \times 120 \times 2 - 7.6 \times 240{/tex}
{tex}= 2040 - 1824{/tex} = 216 MeV

question_text: From the relation R = R 0 A 1/3 where R 0 is a constant and A is the mass number of a nucleus, show that the nuclear matter density is nearly constant (i.e. independent of A).
explanation: The relation between radius and mass number of the nucleus is R =R 0 A 1/3 Where, R 0 = 1.2 fmR = radius of nucleus and A = mass number Nuclear density, {tex}\rho = \frac { \text { Mass of nucleus } } { \text { Volume of nucleus } } = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( R _ { 0 } A ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\rho = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } A } \Rightarrow \rho = \frac { m } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} So as per above formula, density of nucleus does not depend on mass number of nucleus rather it is same for all the atoms and it is roughly in the order of 10 17 kg/m 3 which is very large as compared to our everyday observed densities.
solution: The relation between radius and mass number of the nucleus is R =R 0 A 1/3 Where, R 0 = 1.2 fmR = radius of nucleus and A = mass number Nuclear density, {tex}\rho = \frac { \text { Mass of nucleus } } { \text { Volume of nucleus } } = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( R _ { 0 } A ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\rho = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } A } \Rightarrow \rho = \frac { m } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} So as per above formula, density of nucleus does not depend on mass number of nucleus rather it is same for all the atoms and it is roughly in the order of 10 17 kg/m 3 which is very large as compared to our everyday observed densities.
solution_html:
The relation between radius and mass number of the nucleus is R =R0 A1/3
Where, R0 = 1.2 fmR = radius of nucleus and A = mass number
Nuclear density,

{tex}\rho = \frac { \text { Mass of nucleus } } { \text { Volume of nucleus } } = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( R _ { 0 } A ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}\rho = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } A } \Rightarrow \rho = \frac { m } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
So as per above formula, density of nucleus does not depend on mass number of nucleus rather it is same for all the atoms and it is roughly in the order of 1017 kg/m3 which is very large as compared to our everyday observed densities.

  2. Draw a plot of potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation. Write two important conclusions that can be drawn from the graph.
question_text: Write three characteristic properties of nuclear force. Draw a plot of potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation. Write two important conclusions that can be drawn from the graph.
explanation: The nuclear force binds nucleons into atomic nuclei. Characteristics properties of nuclear force are: Nuclear forces act between a pair of neutrons, a pair of protons and also between a neutron-proton pair, with the same strength. This shows that nuclear forces are independent of charge. The nuclear forces are dependent on spin or angular momentum of nuclei. Nuclear forces are non-central forces. This shows that the distribution of nucleons in a nucleus is not spherically symmetric. A plot of potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation is shown below: From the plot, it is concluded that The potential energy is minimum at a distance {tex}r _ { 0 } ( \approx 0.8 \mathrm { fm } ){/tex} which means that the force is attractive for distances larger than 0.8 fm and repulsive for the distance less than 0.8 fm between the nucleons Nuclear forces are negligible when the distances between the nucleons is more than 10 fm.

1. The nuclear force binds nucleons into atomic nuclei. Characteristics properties of nuclear force are:
    1. Nuclear forces act between a pair of neutrons, a pair of protons and also between a neutron-proton pair, with the same strength. This shows that nuclear forces are independent of charge.
    2. The nuclear forces are dependent on spin or angular momentum of nuclei.
    3. Nuclear forces are non-central forces. This shows that the distribution of nucleons in a nucleus is not spherically symmetric.
  2. A plot of potential energy of a pair of nucleons as a function of their separation is shown below:

    From the plot, it is concluded that 
    1. The potential energy is minimum at a distance {tex}r _ { 0 } ( \approx 0.8 \mathrm { fm } ){/tex} which means that the force is attractive for distances larger than 0.8 fm and repulsive for the distance less than 0.8 fm between the nucleons
    2. Nuclear forces are negligible when the distances between the nucleons is more than 10 fm.

Nuclear fusion is a process of a combination of two light nuclei to form heavier nuclei. It generally occurs in elements of low atomic mass. If the combined nuclear mass is less than that of iron at the peak of the binding energy curve, then the nuclear particles will be more tightly bound than they were in the lighter nuclei, and that decrease in mass comes off in the form of energy according to the Einstein relationship. e.g. {tex}_1^1H + _1^1H \to _1^2He + {e^ + } + v + 0.42MeV{/tex} {tex}_1^2H + _1^2H \to _1^3He + n + 3.27MeV{/tex} {tex}_ { 1 } ^ { 2 } H + _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } H \longrightarrow _ { 1 } H ^ { 3 } + _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } H + 4.03 M e ^ { V }{/tex} According to the question, {tex}_ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { H } + \mathrm { H } \longrightarrow _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } \mathrm { He } + n \mathrm { MeV }{/tex} {tex}\Delta m = ( 2014102 + 3.016049 ){/tex} {tex}- ( 4.002603 + 1.008665 ){/tex} = 0.018883.u Energy released, {tex}Q = 0.018883 \times 9315 \frac { \mathrm { MeV } } { c ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 17.589 MeV
solution_html:
Nuclear fission is a process of splitting of a heavier nucleus into two lighter nuclei. It generally occurs in elements of high atomic mass. When these radioactive nuclides dissociate the total binding energy of product is less than that of reactants which is released energy in radioactive decay.
{tex}\mathrm { U } _ { 92 } ^ { 235 } + n _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \longrightarrow \mathrm { Ba } _ { 56 } ^ { 141 } + \mathrm { Kr } _ { 36 } ^ { 92 } + 3 n _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + Q{/tex}
In this case, the energy released per fission of {tex}U _ { 92 } ^ { 215 }{/tex} is 200.4 MeV.
Nuclear fusion is a process of a combination of two light nuclei to form heavier nuclei. It generally occurs in elements of low atomic mass. If the combined nuclear mass is less than that of iron at the peak of the binding energy curve, then the nuclear particles will be more tightly bound than they were in the lighter nuclei, and that decrease in mass comes off in the form of energy according to the Einstein relationship.
e.g. {tex}_1^1H + _1^1H \to _1^2He + {e^ + } + v + 0.42MeV{/tex}
{tex}_1^2H + _1^2H \to _1^3He + n + 3.27MeV{/tex}
{tex}_ { 1 } ^ { 2 } H + _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } H \longrightarrow _ { 1 } H ^ { 3 } + _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } H + 4.03 M e ^ { V }{/tex}
According to the question,
{tex}_ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { H } + \mathrm { H } \longrightarrow _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } \mathrm { He } + n \mathrm { MeV }{/tex}
{tex}\Delta m = ( 2014102 + 3.016049 ){/tex}{tex}- ( 4.002603 + 1.008665 ){/tex}
= 0.018883.u
Energy released, {tex}Q = 0.018883 \times 9315 \frac { \mathrm { MeV } } { c ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
= 17.589 MeV

question_text: How the size of a nucleus is experimentally determined? Write the relation between the radius and mass number of the nucleus. Show that the density of the nucleus is independent of its mass number.
explanation: Radius of nucleus is determined by Rutherford α−particle scattering experiment by using the concept of distance of closest approach. The relation between radius and mass number of the nucleus is R = R 0 A 1/3 where, R 0 = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 is the range of nuclear force, R = radius of nucleus and A = mass number The expression for radius of nucleus can be used to compute the density of the nucleus. Let us find the density of the nucleus of an atom, whose mass number is A. Now, Density of the nucleus, {tex}\rho = \frac { \text { Mass of nucleus } } { \text { Volume of nucleus } } = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi \left( R _ { 0 } A ^ { 1 / 3 } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\rho = \frac { m A } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } A } \Rightarrow \rho = \frac { m } { \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } }{/tex} So as per above formula, density of nucleus does not depend on mass number of nucleus rather it is same for all the atoms and it is roughly in the order of 10 17 kg/m 3 which is very large as compared to our everyday observed densities.

  2. When a heavy nucleus with mass number A = 240 breaks into two nuclei, A = 120, energy is released in the process.
  3. In β-decay, the experimental detection of neutrinos (or antineutrinos) is found to be extremely difficult.
question_text: Why is the binding energy per nucleon found to be constant for nuclei in the range of mass number (A) lying between 30 and 170? When a heavy nucleus with mass number A = 240 breaks into two nuclei, A = 120, energy is released in the process. In β-decay, the experimental detection of neutrinos (or antineutrinos) is found to be extremely difficult.
explanation: Figure is a plot of the binding energy per nucleon E bn versus the mass number A for a large number of nuclei. Larger the binding energy per nucleon, the greater the work that must be done to remove the nucleon from the nucleus, the more stable the nucleus. The binding energy per nucleon for the nucleus of range, 30 < A < 170 is close to its maximum value. So, the nucleus belongs to this region is highly stable and does not show radioactivity. Binding energy per nucleon is smaller for heavier nuclei than the middle ones, i.e. heavier nuclei are less stable. When a heavier nucleus such as a nucleus of mass number 240 splits into lighter nuclei (mass number 120), the BE/nucleon changes from about 7.6 MeV to 8.4 MeV. Greater BE of the product nuclei results in the liberation of energy. Neutrinos are neutral particles with very small mass compared to electrons. They have only weak interaction with other particles, therefore, they are very difficult to detect, since they can penetrate large quantity of matter without any interaction.

question_text: What characteristic property of nuclear force explains the constancy of binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) in the range of mass number A lying 30< A<170? Show that the density of nucleus over a wide range of nuclei is constant and independent of mass number A.
explanation: The characteristic property of nuclear force that explains the constancy of binding energy per nucleon is the saturation or short range nature of nuclear forces. In heavy nuclei, nuclear size > a range of nuclear force. Using the formula for the radius of the nucleon, we have R = {tex}\mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{A}^\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let, m be the mass of a nucleon, therefore, density, {tex}\rho=\frac{\mathrm{mA}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{A}^{1 / 3}\right)^{3}}=\frac{\mathrm{mA}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}_{0}^{3} \mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}_{0}^{3}}{/tex} Thus, we can see that density is constant and independent of mass number A.

Due to this, the mass of the nucleus formed is always less than the sum of the masses of the constituent nucleons.

question_html: If the total number of neutrons and protons in a nuclear reaction is conserved, how then is the energy absorbed or evolved in the reaction?
question_text: If the total number of neutrons and protons in a nuclear reaction is conserved, how then is the energy absorbed or evolved in the reaction?
explanation: The total rest mass of protons and neutrons is same on the two sides of the nuclear reaction as their number is conserved. But the total binding energy of the nuclei on the two sides need not be equal. The difference in these energies appears as the energy released or absorbed (Q-value of the reaction).
solution: The total rest mass of protons and neutrons is same on the two sides of the nuclear reaction as their number is conserved. question_html: What is nuclear fusion? Explain, how such a large amount of energy is produced inside the sun through proton-proton cycle and carbon-carbon cycle.
question_text: What is nuclear fusion? Explain, how such a large amount of energy is produced inside the sun through proton-proton cycle and carbon-carbon cycle.
explanation: Nuclear fusion is the process by which two light atomic nuclei combine to form a single heavier one while releasing massive amounts of energy. Inside the Sun, this process begins with protons (which is simply a lone hydrogen nucleus) and through a series of steps, these protons fuse together and are turned into helium. This fusion process occurs inside the core of the Sun, and the transformation results in a release of energy that keeps the sun hot.
solution: Nuclear fusion is the process by which two light atomic nuclei combine to form a single heavier one while releasing massive amounts of energy. This fusion process occurs inside the core of the Sun, and the transformation results in a release of energy that keeps the sun hot.

question_html: What are nuclear forces? Give their important properties.
question_text: What are nuclear forces? Give their important properties.
explanation: The strong attractive forces acting between the protons and neutrons of a nucleus which keep them bound together inside the tiny nucleus are called nuclear forces. The important properties of nuclear forces are as follows: Nuclear forces are the strongest attractive forces known in nature F G : F E : F N = 1 :10 36 : 10 38 . They are short range forces effective upto 2 - 3 fermi from a nucleon. They have charge independent nature. They show saturation effect i.e., a nucleon can interact only with a neighbouring nucleon. They are non-central forces i.e., they do not act along the line joining the centres of the two nucleons.
solution: The strong attractive forces acting between the protons and neutrons of a nucleus which keep them bound together inside the tiny nucleus are called nuclear forces. The important properties of nuclear forces are as follows: Nuclear forces are the strongest attractive forces known in nature F G : F E : F N = 1 :10 36 : 10 38 . They are short range forces effective upto 2 - 3 fermi from a nucleon. They have charge independent nature. They show saturation effect i.e., a nucleon can interact only with a neighbouring nucleon. They are non-central forces i.e., they do not act along the line joining the centres of the two nucleons.
solution_html:
The strong attractive forces acting between the protons and neutrons of a nucleus which keep them bound together inside the tiny nucleus are called nuclear forces. The important properties of nuclear forces are as follows:

  1. Nuclear forces are the strongest attractive forces known in nature FG : FE : FN = 1 :1036 : 1038.
  2. They are short range forces effective upto 2 - 3 fermi from a nucleon.
  3. They have charge independent nature.
  4. They show saturation effect i.e., a nucleon can interact only with a neighbouring nucleon.
  5. They are non-central forces i.e., they do not act along the line joining the centres of the two nucleons.

or M2 - 20(mp + mn) > 2M1 - 20(mp + mn)
or M2 > 2M1

question_html: Describe how Chadwick discovered neutrons. Is neutron a stable particle when isolated?
question_text: Describe how Chadwick discovered neutrons. Is neutron a stable particle when isolated?
explanation: The neutrons were discovered by James Chadwick in 1932. He was awarded the 1935 Nobel prize for physics for this discovery. In 1932, Chadwick performed an experiment in which {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles from a radioactive Polonium source were used to bombard beryllium nuclei. Highly penetrating rays were found to come out of the beryllium metal, which could not be deflected by electric and magnetic fields. These radiations were used to bombard hydrocarbons like paraffin wax. High energy protons were knocked out from the paraffin wax. The energy of the ejected protons was found to be too high to be accounted for y-ray photons By using the laws of conservation of energy and momentum, Chadwick concluded that the penetrating radiation consisted of neutral particles, each having a mass nearly that of a proton. These particles were called neutrons. A free or isolated neutron is unstable because A free neutron spontaneously decays into a proton, electron and antineutron.
solution: The neutrons were discovered by James Chadwick in 1932. He was awarded the 1935 Nobel prize for physics for this discovery. In 1932, Chadwick performed an experiment in which {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles from a radioactive Polonium source were used to bombard beryllium nuclei. Highly penetrating rays were found to come out of the beryllium metal, which could not be deflected by electric and magnetic fields. These radiations were used to bombard hydrocarbons like paraffin wax. High energy protons were knocked out from the paraffin wax. The energy of the ejected protons was found to be too high to be accounted for y-ray photons By using the laws of conservation of energy and momentum, Chadwick concluded that the penetrating radiation consisted of neutral particles, each having a mass nearly that of a proton. These particles were called neutrons. A free or isolated neutron is unstable because A free neutron spontaneously decays into a proton, electron and antineutron.
solution_html:
The neutrons were discovered by James Chadwick in 1932. He was awarded the 1935 Nobel prize for physics for this discovery.

In 1932, Chadwick performed an experiment in which {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles from a radioactive Polonium source were used to bombard beryllium nuclei. Highly penetrating rays were found to come out of the beryllium metal, which could not be deflected by electric and magnetic fields. These radiations were used to bombard hydrocarbons like paraffin wax. High energy protons were knocked out from the paraffin wax. The energy of the ejected protons was found to be too high to be accounted for y-ray photons By using the laws of conservation of energy and momentum, Chadwick concluded that the penetrating radiation consisted of neutral particles, each having a mass nearly that of a proton. These particles were called neutrons. 
A free or isolated neutron is unstable because A free neutron spontaneously decays into a proton, electron and antineutron.
